CHIJ St Nicholas Girls' School — P6 Science Mid-Year Assessment (SA1) 2022

Total Time: 1 hour 45 minutes  |  Booklet A: 56 marks (Q1–Q28, MCQ)  |  Booklet B: 44 marks (Q29–Q40, open-ended)

Booklet A — Multiple-Choice Questions (28 × 2 marks = 56 marks)

Question 1

2 marks

Study the classification chart below.

Which of the following best represents A, B and C?

ABC
(1)Does it have 6 legs?Does it have moist skin?lizard
(2)Does it have wings?Does it reproduce by laying eggs?dog
(3)Does it have a hard outer covering?Does it have scales?frog
(4)Does it have 3 body parts?Does it have a 3-stage life cycle?cockroach

Answer

Option 3. "Cat" branches off at "Does it have hair?" (a mammal). A must separate the next group (grasshopper, an insect) — "Does it have a hard outer covering?" fits an insect's exoskeleton. After "Does it have feathers?" (ostrich, a bird), B must split the remaining animals using a reptile/amphibian trait — "Does it have scales?" — and the "no" answer C is then the frog (an amphibian, which has no scales). Options 1, 2 and 4 give traits or final animals that do not match the yes/no path of the chart.

Question 2

2 marks

Which of the following plant parts is correctly matched to its function?

Plant partFunction
(1)leavestake in water for the plant
(2)fruitsattract animals to eat them
(3)rootshold the plant firmly to the ground
(4)stemtransports oxygen to all parts of the plant

Answer

Option 3. Roots anchor and hold the plant firmly in the ground (and absorb water and minerals). Option 1 is wrong — roots, not leaves, take in water. Option 2 is wrong — fruits protect the seeds and aid seed dispersal; it is flowers that attract animals/insects. Option 4 is wrong — the stem transports water and food, not oxygen.

Question 3

2 marks

The diagram below shows a plant at different stages of its development.

The stages are labelled A, B, C and D (left to right). Which of the following shows the correct order of its development?

  1. A, B, C, D
  2. B, A, D, C
  3. C, D, B, A
  4. D, A, C, B

Answer

Option 4 (D, A, C, B). This is the answer given in the school's official answer key. Note for learners: the four stages are A = fruiting plant, B = young leafy plant, C = seedling and D = flowering plant, so the natural order of growth is seedling → young leafy plant → flowering plant → fruiting plant, i.e. C, B, D, A. That sequence is not offered as an option, so the question as set is flawed; the marked answer is option 4.

Question 4

2 marks

Study the cells shown in the diagram below.

Which of the following statements is definitely true?

  1. Cells X, Y and Z are unicellular organisms.
  2. Only cell Y cannot maintain a regular shape.
  3. Only cell X is a plant cell as it has chloroplasts.
  4. Both cells X and Z can make food as they have a cell wall.

Answer

Option 2. Cell Y is the rounded cell with no cell wall, so it cannot keep a fixed, regular shape — this is definitely true. Option 1 is wrong: these are single cells, not necessarily whole unicellular organisms. Option 3 is wrong: having a regular box-like shape with a cell wall shows X and Z are plant cells, but the statement claims only X — and it is the cell wall (not chloroplasts) that is seen here. Option 4 is wrong: a cell wall does not let a cell make food; only cells with chlorophyll/chloroplasts can photosynthesise.

Question 5

2 marks

The heart rate of a person before, during and after a strenuous activity was measured and recorded in the graph as shown below. Points A, B, C and D are marked on the graph.

Which of the points A, B, C or D correctly shows when she started and ended the strenuous activity?

Started strenuous activityEnded strenuous activity
(1)AC
(2)AD
(3)BC
(4)BD

Answer

Option 1. Heart rate stays low and steady until point A, then rises as the activity begins — so she started at A. The heart rate stays high through B and C, then begins to fall after C as she stops exerting — so she ended the strenuous activity at C. B is during the activity and D is during recovery, so options 2, 3 and 4 are wrong.

Question 6

2 marks

The diagram below shows two fruits, a papaya and an avocado.

The table below shows the classification of flowers W and X.

One ovuleMany ovules
WX

Which of the following best represents the flower the fruit may be developed from?

Papaya fruitAvocado fruit
(1)WW
(2)WX
(3)XX
(4)XW

Answer

Option 4. Each ovule develops into one seed. The papaya has many seeds, so it came from a flower with many ovules — flower X. The avocado has only one seed, so it came from a flower with one ovule — flower W. Therefore papaya = X and avocado = W.

Question 7

2 marks

At the beginning, island X had plants and animals, but island Y had no plants and no animals.

A few years later, island Y started to have plants and the distribution of young plants is shown in the diagram below.

What are the likely fruit dispersal methods of these plants?

  1. wind and water
  2. wind and animal
  3. water and animal
  4. wind, water and animal

Answer

Option 2 (wind and animal). The young plants are spread fairly evenly across the inland area, not just along the shoreline — so the seeds did not arrive only by water (water-dispersed seeds would be concentrated near the coast). Seeds reaching the whole island, including the centre, are most likely carried there by wind and by animals (such as birds) that travelled from island X. Hence wind and animal dispersal.

Question 8

2 marks

The diagram below shows the human male and female reproductive systems. Parts are labelled A to F.

Which one of the following statements is true?

  1. Parts C and F produce the egg cells.
  2. The egg cell travels through part A to part B.
  3. Male reproductive cells are produced at part C.
  4. The male reproductive cells are deposited at part B by part E.

Answer

Option 3. Part C is the testis, where the male reproductive cells (sperm) are produced. Option 1 is wrong — egg cells are produced in the ovaries (in the female system), not in C and F. Option 2 is wrong — it describes the wrong path/parts for the egg cell. Option 4 is wrong — it names the wrong parts for where the sperm are deposited.

Question 9

2 marks

A leaf of a plant was covered with clear plastic sheets as shown below and left in a dark room for a day.

The plant was then placed under bright sunlight for six hours. The white portion of the leaf does not contain any chlorophyll. Four discs P, Q, R and S were punched out from the leaf in the positions shown above and a starch test was conducted. Iodine solution changes from yellowish-brown to blue-black in the presence of starch.

Key: filled circle = blue-black, open circle = yellowish-brown.

Which one of the following shows the correct colour change of the iodine solution?

(Options are numbered 1, 2, 3, 4 — top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right.)

Answer

Option 4. A disc turns blue-black only if it made starch, which needs BOTH light AND chlorophyll. A disc stays yellowish-brown if it was covered (no light) or lay on the white, chlorophyll-free part. The correct option shows blue-black only for the disc(s) that received light and contained chlorophyll, and yellowish-brown for the covered disc and the disc on the white region — this matches option 4.

Question 10

2 marks

The graph below shows how the average size of the stomata of a plant that was placed in an open field changes at different times of the day. The size of stomata increases when a plant photosynthesises.

Based on the graph above, which one of the following statement(s) is / are most likely true?

A   No photosynthesis occurred after 11 pm.
B   The rate of photosynthesis increased from 3 am to 7 am.
C   The rate of photosynthesis decreased the fastest from 7 pm to 11 pm.
D   The plant was photosynthesising at its maximum rate from 11 am to 3 pm.

  1. D only
  2. A and C only
  3. B and D only
  4. All of the above

Answer

Option 3 (B and D only). B is true — stomata size rises from 3 am to 7 am, so the rate of photosynthesis increased. D is true — stomata size is at its highest and constant from 11 am to 3 pm, so photosynthesis was at its maximum rate. A is false — the graph cannot show "no photosynthesis"; the stomata size after 11 pm is small but the statement is not supported. C is false — the steepest fall is not in the 7 pm–11 pm interval as claimed.

Question 11

2 marks

The pie charts below show the different populations of organisms found in two different communities K and L.

Which of the following statement(s) about communities K and L is / are false?

A   The number of organism T in both communities are the same.
B   The percentage of organism U in both communities are the same.
C   There are lesser populations of organisms in community K than in L.
D   The total number of organisms in community L is lesser than in community K.

  1. B only
  2. A and B only
  3. C and D only
  4. A, C and D only

Answer

Option 4 (A, C and D only). The pie charts only show percentages, not actual numbers, so we cannot compare the total or actual numbers of organisms between the two communities. A is false (we cannot say T's numbers are equal — only the percentages 45% are equal), C is false and D is false (we cannot compare total numbers at all). B is true — organism U is 15% in both communities, so the percentages really are the same. Hence the false statements are A, C and D.

Question 12

2 marks

The diagram below shows a pond containing plant A (a floating plant), plant B (a submerged plant) and fish.

The graph below shows how the population of plant A changes over a period of time.

Which of the following shows the correct observation and explanation of how the population size of plant B will change over the same period of time?

Population size of plant BReason
(1)IncreaseThe fishes ate plant A.
(2)IncreasePlant B could make more food.
(3)DecreasePlant B did not receive enough sunlight.
(4)DecreaseThe temperature of the water decreased.

Answer

Option 3. As the floating plant A increases, it covers more of the water surface and blocks sunlight from reaching the submerged plant B below. With less sunlight, plant B photosynthesises less, makes less food and grows poorly, so its population decreases. The reason in option 3 (not enough sunlight) is correct; options 1, 2 and 4 give wrong causes.

Question 13

2 marks

The table below shows the masses of four different materials A, B, C and D, before and after being completely submerged in a container of water for 10 minutes.

MaterialOriginal mass (g)Mass after 10 minutes (g)
A3139
B2545
C80160
D5560

Which of the following shows the correct order of the materials from the least absorbent to the most absorbent?

Least →→ Most
(1)CBAD
(2)ADBC
(3)CDBA
(4)DABC

Answer

Option 4. Absorbency is shown by the mass of water gained: A gained 8 g, B gained 20 g, C gained 80 g, D gained 5 g. From least to most water absorbed: D (5 g) < A (8 g) < B (20 g) < C (80 g). This matches option 4 (D, A, B, C).

Question 14

2 marks

Katie followed the steps below to separate three substances that were mixed together.

Step 1: Add water to the mixture and stir it. Only one of the substances dissolved in water.
Step 2: Pour the mixture through a filter paper to separate the undissolved solids from the liquid.
Step 3: A magnet was placed over the mixture of undissolved solids.
Step 4: The liquid was heated in a beaker.

Katie observed that the magnet attracted some of the undissolved solids. She also noted that a white substance remained in the beaker after the water had completely evaporated.

Which of the following are possible substances found in the original mixture?

  1. Iron, steel and salt
  2. Steel, plastic and sand
  3. Iron, aluminium and salt
  4. Steel, aluminium and sand

Answer

Option 3. One substance dissolved in water and left a white solid when the water evaporated → this is salt. The magnet attracted some of the undissolved solids → a magnetic material such as iron. The third substance is an undissolved, non-magnetic solid such as aluminium. Iron + aluminium + salt fits all the observations. (Options with two magnetic metals, or with sand instead of a soluble salt, do not fit the white-residue clue.)

Question 15

2 marks

Diagram 1 below shows a toy frog W with an iron bar inside it. Diagram 2 shows the cross-section views of two more toy frogs X and Y, each containing a magnet.

Ravi placed rods A and B above the frogs and recorded his observations in the table below.

toy frogs picked up
Rod AX and Y only
Rod BW and Y only

Which of the following shows what rods A and B could be?

rod Arod B
(1)S N (magnet)N S (magnet)
(2)iron bariron bar
(3)iron barS N (magnet)
(4)iron barN S (magnet)

Answer

Option 4. Rod A picked up X and Y (the two magnetic frogs that contain magnets) but NOT W (the iron-bar frog). An iron bar is attracted to a magnet, so an iron-bar rod would be attracted to the magnet frogs X and Y but could not, by itself, lift the iron-bar frog W — this fits rod A being an iron bar. Rod B picked up W and Y but not X, meaning it attracts the iron bar (W) and one polarity of magnet but repels the other — so rod B must be a magnet with poles oriented (N S) so that it attracts Y but repels X. Only option 4 has rod A = iron bar and rod B = a correctly-oriented magnet.

Question 16

2 marks

The experiment below is carried out in a dark room. Sheets A, B, C and D are arranged in a straight line and there is a circular hole on sheet B. When the torch is switched on, a bright circular patch of light is seen on sheet C only.

Which of the following correctly describes the properties of the materials A, B, C and D?

Allows light to pass throughDoes not allow light to pass throughNot possible to tell
(1)ACB and D
(2)AB and CD
(3)B and CAD
(4)C and DBA

Answer

Option 2. Light from the torch must pass through sheet A to reach the hole in B, so A allows light to pass through (transparent/translucent). A bright patch forms on C and stops there — C blocks the light, so C does not allow light to pass through (opaque). B is opaque except for its hole, so B also does not allow light through (the light only got past via the hole). Since no light reaches D, we cannot tell D's property. This matches option 2: A passes light; B and C block light; D — not possible to tell.

Question 17

2 marks

Which of the following correctly shows the path of light that makes it possible for the girl to see the fan?

(Options are numbered 1, 2, 3, 4 — top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right.)

Answer

Option 2. The fan does not give out its own light. To see it, light from the lamp must first travel to the fan, then be reflected off the fan into the girl's eye. Option 2 correctly shows light going from the lamp to the fan and then from the fan to the girl's eye. The other options show light travelling directly into the eye or in directions that would not let the fan be seen.

Question 18

2 marks

Water at room temperature was poured into two identical beakers P and Q. Both beakers were heated. The temperatures of water in P and Q were recorded every minute until water in both beakers boiled.

Which of the following shows the correct graph for the above observation?

(Options are numbered 1, 2, 3, 4 — top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right.)

Answer

Option 3. Beaker P holds more water than Q, so P needs more heat and takes longer to reach boiling point (100°C). Both start at the same room temperature and both level off at 100°C when boiling. The correct graph shows Q rising faster and reaching 100°C before P, with both flattening at 100°C — this is option 3 (Q above/ahead of P, both ending at 100°C).

Question 19

2 marks

Which of the following about heat is true?

  1. Heat is a matter.
  2. Heat is a form of energy.
  3. Heat travels from cold to hot objects.
  4. Heat can be measured using a thermometer.

Answer

Option 2. Heat is a form of energy. Option 1 is wrong — heat is not matter (it has no mass or volume). Option 3 is wrong — heat always travels from a hotter object to a colder object, not the reverse. Option 4 is wrong — a thermometer measures temperature, not the amount of heat.

Question 20

2 marks

More air was pumped into a fully inflated rubber ball.

Which of the following shows the correct observation about the mass and volume of the rubber ball?

MassVolume
(1)increaseincrease
(2)increaseremains the same
(3)remains the sameincrease
(4)remains the sameremains the same

Answer

Option 2. Air has mass, so pumping in more air increases the ball's mass. Because the ball is already fully inflated, its rubber casing cannot stretch any further, so the volume stays the same — the extra air is simply compressed inside. Hence mass increases while volume remains the same.

Question 21

2 marks

The table below shows the properties of P, Q, R and S. A tick (✓) shows that the property is present.

PropertyPQRS
Has mass✓✓✓
Has a definite shape✓✓
Has a definite volume✓

Which of the following best represents plasticine and a shadow?

PlasticineShadow
(1)QP
(2)QS
(3)RP
(4)RS

Answer

Option 4. Plasticine is a solid, so it has mass, a definite shape and a definite volume (moulding it changes its shape but not its volume) — all three properties are present → R. A shadow is not matter at all: it has no mass, no shape of its own and no volume → no properties → S. Therefore plasticine = R and shadow = S.

Question 22

2 marks

The table below shows the melting and boiling points of substances X and Y.

SubstanceXY
Melting point (°C)160120
Boiling point (°C)430300

At which temperature are substances X and Y in the same state of matter?

  1. 140°C
  2. 170°C
  3. 380°C
  4. 420°C

Answer

Option 2 (170°C). A substance is a liquid between its melting and boiling points. At 170°C: X (m.p. 160, b.p. 430) is above its melting point but below its boiling point → liquid; Y (m.p. 120, b.p. 300) is also above melting and below boiling → liquid. So both are liquids — the same state. At 140°C, X is still solid while Y is liquid; at 380°C, X is liquid while Y is gas; at 420°C, X is liquid while Y is gas — so only 170°C gives the same state.

Question 23

2 marks

The graph below shows the temperature changes in a beaker of ice over time. Points A, B, C, D, E and F are marked along the curve.

Based on the graph, which of the following statements is true?

  1. Water started to boil at D.
  2. Heat source was removed at C.
  3. There is no heat gain in AB and CD.
  4. Water reached room temperature at E.

Answer

Option 3. The graph is flat (horizontal) along A–B and along C–D, so the temperature stays constant in those two sections – there is no rise (gain) in temperature there because the substance is changing state. Option 1 is wrong: the water boils all along the flat 100°C stretch, so boiling starts at C, not at D. Option 2 is wrong: the temperature only begins to fall after D, so the heat source was removed at D, not at C (from C to D it stays at 100°C). Option 4 is wrong: the graph does not show that the water has cooled to room temperature at E.

Question 24

2 marks

The circuit below consists of identical bulbs and batteries in good working condition. Several switches can be opened or closed.

What is the least number of switches to be closed in order for three bulbs to be lit at the same time?

  1. 1
  2. 2
  3. 3
  4. 4

Answer

Option 2. By choosing the right two switches, current can be completed through three bulbs at once (for example, closing the switch on a branch carrying one bulb together with the switch on a branch carrying two bulbs in series lights three bulbs). Closing just one switch cannot light three bulbs, so the least number needed is 2.

Question 25

2 marks

The diagram below shows a roller coaster in an amusement park. Points P, Q, R and S are marked along the track.

At which point P, Q, R or S does the roller coaster car have the highest kinetic energy as it moves from P to S?

  1. P
  2. Q
  3. R
  4. S

Answer

Option 2 (Q). The car has the most gravitational potential energy at the highest point (P) and the least at the lowest point. As it goes down, potential energy is converted to kinetic energy, so kinetic energy (and speed) is greatest at the lowest point of the track, which is Q. R is partway up another hill and S is higher than Q, so the car is fastest — highest kinetic energy — at Q.

Question 26

2 marks

The diagram below shows a handphone connected to a phone charger that has a solar panel.

Which of the following shows the main energy conversion for the handphone to work?

  1. Heat energy → Electrical energy → Light energy + Sound energy
  2. Light energy → Electrical energy → Heat energy + Light energy + Sound energy
  3. Heat energy → Electrical energy → Heat energy + Light energy + Sound energy
  4. Light energy → Electrical energy → Light energy + Sound energy

Answer

Option 2. The solar panel changes light energy (from the Sun) into electrical energy to charge and run the handphone. The working handphone then gives out heat, light (screen) and sound. So the conversion is Light → Electrical → Heat + Light + Sound, which is option 2. Options that start with heat energy are wrong (the panel uses light, not heat), and option 4 omits the heat produced.

Question 27

2 marks

Which of the following shows the effects of a force?

A   A girl sitting on a chair.
B   A beaker of water gaining heat.
C   A man moulding a piece of clay.
D   A tennis ball changing direction after being hit.

  1. A and B only
  2. C and D only
  3. A, C and D only
  4. All of the above

Answer

Option 3 (A, C and D only). A force can change an object's shape, motion, speed or direction, or hold things in place. A — the girl sitting shows a force (her weight) acting on the chair. C — moulding clay changes its shape (effect of a force). D — the ball changing direction is a change in motion (effect of a force). B is not an effect of a force — gaining heat is about energy/temperature, not force. Hence A, C and D only.

Question 28

2 marks

Lydia compressed two springs X and Y of the same length to the same height as shown below. Spring X is stiffer than spring Y.

Which of the statements below is true?

  1. Both springs X and Y will exert the same amount of elastic spring force.
  2. When released, spring X will exert a greater elastic spring force than spring Y.
  3. When released, more elastic potential energy will be converted to more kinetic energy in spring Y.
  4. Same amount of chemical potential energy is needed to compress both springs to the same height.

Answer

Option 2. Spring X is stiffer, so for the same amount of compression it stores more elastic potential energy and pushes back harder. When released, the stiffer spring X exerts a greater elastic spring force than the softer spring Y. Option 1 is wrong (the forces are not equal), option 3 is wrong (X, being stiffer, releases more energy, not Y), and option 4 is wrong (more energy is needed to compress the stiffer spring X).

Booklet B — Open-Ended Questions (44 marks)

Question 29

3 marks

Gerald wanted to find out how the breathing rate during different activities is affected by the mass of a person. He invited three friends for the experiment and ensured that each of them took a five-minute break before taking part in the next activity. He recorded the results in the table below.

ActivityFriend X (70 kg)Friend Y (80 kg)Friend Z (90 kg)
Sitting142025
Walking253042
Running404859

(Breathing rate is measured in breaths per minute.)

(a) Explain why their breathing rates were the highest when they were running. [1]
(b) Why did each person take a five-minute break between each activity? [1]
(c) Based on the results, what can Gerald conclude from the experiment? [1]

Answer

(a) Running is the most vigorous activity, so the body needs the most energy. The person breathes fastest to take in the most oxygen and remove the most carbon dioxide for the most respiration.
(b) To allow each person's breathing rate to return to his/her normal resting breathing rate before the next activity, so the results are fair.
(c) The greater the mass of the person, the higher the breathing rate during the same activity.

Question 30

5 marks
(a) Which factors are needed for germination? [1]

Tricia wanted to find out if germination of seeds of plant G is affected by the surrounding temperature. She placed six dishes with 15 seeds in each dish in six different boxes. The boxes were maintained at different temperatures.

The graph below shows the number of seeds that have germinated after 3 days.

(b) List one other condition that should be kept constant. [1]
(c) Based on the graph, what is the relationship between temperature and the number of seeds that have germinated? [2]
(d) Tricia wanted to conduct another experiment to find out if the amount of space provided affects the growth of young plants. List two changes to be made to the above experiment. [1]

Answer

(a) Water, oxygen (air) and warmth.
(b) The amount of water given to each dish of seeds (kept the same).
(c) As the temperature increases up to 23°C, the number of seeds that germinate increases. After 23°C, as the temperature increases further, the number of seeds that germinate decreases.
(d) Keep the temperature of each set-up the same (no longer change it), and change the size of each dish / change the amount of space (number of seeds) in each dish so the variable being tested is the space provided.

Question 31

5 marks

Bertha set up the following experiments in her classroom as shown below. She measured the rate of photosynthesis by counting the number of bubbles produced by water plant W in each set-up for 10 minutes. Baking soda reacts with water to produce carbon dioxide.

Set-up A: no baking soda  |  B: 1 scoop  |  C: 2 scoops  |  D: 3 scoops of baking soda added

(a) State a possible hypothesis for Bertha's experiment. [1]
(b) What is the purpose of set-up A? [1]

The table below shows the results of Bertha's experiment.

Set-upNumber of bubbles produced in 10 minutes
A10
B22
C35
D47

Organism P (shown below) grows poorly in waters with high levels of carbon dioxide. To help organism P grow well, fishermen usually grow some plant Q next to a cage of P as shown below.

(c) Based on Bertha's experiment, explain how plant Q is able to help organism P grow well in waters with high levels of carbon dioxide. [2]

Answer

(a) The greater the amount of carbon dioxide (more scoops of baking soda), the higher the rate of photosynthesis / the greater the number of bubbles produced.
(b) Set-up A acts as the control set-up, to show that any change in the number of bubbles produced is due to the amount of baking soda (carbon dioxide) added and not to other factors.
(c) Plant Q photosynthesises and uses up carbon dioxide. In waters with high levels of carbon dioxide, plant Q can photosynthesise faster and take in more carbon dioxide, reducing the amount of carbon dioxide in the water. This allows organism P to grow well.

Question 32

4 marks

Study the diagram below. Human waste drops into the container containing bacteria P. After some time, process X occurs and a soil-like substance is produced. Gas Q is produced during process X.

(a) Describe the role of bacteria P in process X. [1]
(b) State what is gas Q. [1]
(c) Other than the presence of water and air, state another environmental factor that will affect the speed of process X. [1]
(d) The soil-like substance can be used as fertilisers for the plants. Why is it important to the environment to use the soil-like substance as fertilisers for plants? [1]

Answer

(a) Bacteria P is a decomposer that breaks down the human waste/waste materials into simpler substances.
(b) Carbon dioxide.
(c) Temperature / the amount of warmth.
(d) It replaces the nutrients/minerals in the soil that have been used up, providing additional nutrients to plants, and it recycles waste so less waste pollutes the environment.

Question 33

4 marks

Ali held two toy trucks 10 cm away from each other as shown in diagram 1 below. When he released the two trucks, they moved away from each other. Each truck carries a bar (bar X) and one carries magnet A.

(a) State the force that caused the observation in diagram 1 above. [1]
(b) Without making any changes to the objects used above, suggest a way to make the trucks move away from each other faster. [1]

Ali replaced bar X with bar P and repeated the experiment. He noted that the trucks moved towards each other as shown in diagram 2.

(c) He concluded that bars X and P are both magnets. Do you agree with him? Explain your answer. [2]

Answer

(a) Magnetic force of repulsion.
(b) Push the two trucks closer to each other before releasing them (a smaller distance gives a stronger magnetic force, so they move apart faster).
(c) No. Bar X must be a magnet, because like poles of X and magnet A faced each other and repelled (only two magnets can repel). However, bar P only needs to be attracted to magnet A — so bar P could be a magnet OR simply a magnetic material (such as iron). Therefore we cannot conclude that bar P is definitely a magnet.

Question 34

3 marks

The diagram below shows objects A and B made of wood and frosted glass respectively.

Objects A and B were used in the experiment shown below.

(a) A shadow was cast on the screen. Draw the shadow in the box below. [1]
(b) State a property of light that enables shadows to be formed. [1]
(c) A change was made to the experiment. It was observed that the shadow became smaller although the reading on the light sensor remained the same. What was the change? [1]

Answer

(a) Only object A (the opaque wooden cube) casts a clear shadow on the screen — a 5 cm square. Object B is made of frosted glass, which lets light pass through, so it does not cast a clear shadow. The shadow drawn is a square:
(b) Light travels in a straight line (and cannot pass through the opaque object), so a shadow is formed behind it.
(c) Both objects A and B were moved closer to the screen. (Moving the opaque object nearer the screen makes its shadow smaller, while the total amount of light reaching the sensor stays the same.)

Question 35

3 marks

Ben filled a beaker with 20 cm³ of marbles as shown in diagram 1. He added 20 cm³ of water and predicted that the final water level will be as shown in diagram 2.

(a) Do you think his prediction is correct? Explain your answer. [2]
(b) State a property of water that supports your answer in (a). [1]

Answer

(a) No, his prediction is not correct. There are air spaces between the marbles. When water is added, the water flows into and fills these air spaces, so the final water level will be lower than Ben predicted (it will not simply add up to a separate 20 cm³ layer above the marbles).
(b) Water has no definite shape — it takes the shape of its container and flows to fill the gaps between the marbles.

Question 36

4 marks

Albert wanted to find out which container helps to keep water hot for a longer time. He placed a paper cup filled to the brim with boiling water into container G as shown below.

He repeated the experiment with a similar container H made of a different material. He conducted the experiment in a room and recorded the results in the graph shown below.

(a) Why should the size of the paper cup be kept the same? [1]
(b) Which container is able to keep water hot for a longer time? Explain your answer. [2]
(c) Based on the graph, what is the temperature of the room? [1]

Answer

(a) To ensure the amount/volume of water used is the same so that the test is fair, with only one changed variable — the material/type of container.
(b) Container G. The temperature of the water in G decreased more slowly, so G is a poorer conductor of heat. It allows the water to lose heat more slowly to the surroundings, keeping the water hot for a longer time.
(c) 30°C (the temperature the water levels off at once it stops cooling).

Question 37

4 marks

Martha wanted to investigate if a certain factor affects the time taken for t-shirts to dry. She washed and hung two identical t-shirts X and Y to dry as shown below. T-shirt Y is folded in half.

(a) Name and describe the process that allows the t-shirts to dry. [1]

She measured the mass of each shirt every thirty minutes and plotted the results as shown in the graph below. Two boxes are provided on the curves to be labelled.

(b) Complete the graph above by labelling X or Y in the boxes provided. [1]
(c) Explain your answer in (b). [2]

Answer

(a) Evaporation. The water in the t-shirts gains heat from the surrounding (warm) air and changes into water vapour (this can happen at any temperature), so the t-shirts dry.
(b) The upper curve (which loses mass more slowly and stays heavier) is Y; the lower curve (which loses mass faster and becomes lighter sooner) is X:
(c) T-shirt X is spread out, so it has a greater exposed surface area to the surrounding air. Water in shirt X gains heat faster and evaporates faster, so shirt X loses mass faster and dries faster. T-shirt Y is folded in half, so it has a smaller exposed surface area, evaporates more slowly and stays heavier for longer.

Question 38

2 marks

Three materials W, X and Y were used to set up two circuits A and B as shown below.

The two circuits were tested and the results are shown in the table below.

CircuitDoes the bulb light up?
Ano
Byes

Another circuit C was constructed using materials W, X and Y as shown below.

How many bulbs would light up? Explain your answer. [2]

Answer

Zero bulbs would light up. In circuit A the bulb did not light, while in circuit B it did. The difference is material W (in A) versus Y (in B), so W is an electrical insulator and Y is an electrical conductor; X must be a conductor too (it is in both circuits). In circuit C, material W lies on the only path that the current must pass through to reach the rest of the circuit. Since W is an insulator, it breaks the circuit (an open circuit) and electricity cannot flow, so none of the bulbs light up.

Question 39

5 marks

Paul conducted an experiment to find out if the distance moved by an object would be affected by the type of material the object was moving on. For each material used, he gave the object a push with the same amount of force.

The table below shows his results.

Material1st try2nd try3rd tryAverage
E10.110.310.210.2
F13.513.613.213.4
G5.25.15.35.2

(Distance y is measured in cm.)

(a) What is frictional force? [1]
(b) Based on his results, which material, E, F or G, should he use to cover the floor if he wanted to prevent falls? Give a reason for your answer. [2]
(c) Besides frictional force, name one other force acting on the object as it was moving. [1]
(d) Why did Paul repeat his experiments 3 times for each material? [1]

Answer

(a) Frictional force is a type of force that opposes motion (it acts in the opposite direction to the way an object moves).
(b) Material G. The object moved the shortest distance (5.2 cm) on G, which shows there is the greatest/most frictional force between the object and G. G is therefore the least slippery surface, so it is the most suitable to cover the floor to prevent people from slipping/falling.
(c) Gravitational force.
(d) To make the results more reliable / accurate (so a single odd reading does not affect the conclusion).

Question 40

4 marks

Keming carried out an experiment using a catapult. He pulled back the catapult with ball A downwards before releasing it, causing the ball to be launched into the air. Ball A then travelled some distance before falling onto the grass patch.

He measured the distance moved by ball A and recorded his results in his notebook as shown below. He repeated his experiment two more times.

(a) State the main energy conversion that has occurred for ball A to reach point X. [1]
(b) Give a reason why the distance moved by ball A was different for each try. [1]

Keming repeated the experiment with another ball B of a different mass. His results for ball B are shown below.

Ball B (30 g)  —  1st try: 174 cm  |  2nd try: 179 cm  |  3rd try: 177 cm

(c) What is the aim of Keming's experiment? [1]
(d) Keming's father suggested that he should conduct his experiment on a sand pit instead of a grass patch. Do you agree with him? Explain your answer. [1]

Answer

(a) The main energy conversion is elastic potential energy → kinetic energy. Filling in the boxes: Kinetic energy (Keming's hand pulling back) → Elastic potential energy (stored in the stretched catapult) → Kinetic energy → Gravitational potential energy (ball A rising to point X):
(b) The force exerted on the catapult/ball for each try was not exactly the same (and the amount of wind was not constant for each try), so the distance moved by ball A was different each time.
(c) To find out if the mass of the ball affects the distance moved by the ball.
(d) Yes. Conducting the experiment on a sand pit allows the ball to leave a more obvious mark/dent/depression when it lands, so that the distance moved by the ball can be measured more accurately.
